title: Add HTTP security headers middleware (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)
description: No Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, or Permissions-Policy headers are set. This is the single biggest security gap. Add a middleware in main.go that sets: CSP default-src self, HSTS max-age=31536000, X-Content-Type-Options nosniff, X-Frame-Options SAMEORIGIN, Referrer-Policy no-referrer, Permissions-Policy geolocation=() microphone=() camera=(). File: main.go.
close_reason: Added SecurityHeaders middleware with CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy. HSTS only applied in production. Full test coverage.

title: DatastoreDB.UpdateSessionExpiry always fails: uses untagged Session struct instead of SessionEntity
description: UpdateSessionExpiry (internal/database/datastore.go) loaded/saved sessions using the bare Session struct (schema.go), which has no datastore struct tags, so its implicit property names (ID, UserID, CreatedAt, ExpiresAt) don't match the tagged property names (user_id, created_at, expires_at) written by CreateSession via SessionEntity. Every call to UpdateSessionExpiry against a session created through the normal CreateSession path fails with 'datastore: cannot load field "expires_at" into a "database.Session": no such struct field'. Found via emulator-backed test while implementing gr-4avl.
close_reason: Already fixed in commit 198446a (UpdateSessionExpiry now uses SessionEntity). Re-closing: prior close in this same session didn't get persisted to .beads/issues.jsonl before commit.

title: SetAdminStatus can never revoke admin (is_admin:false rejected by binding:required)
description: internal/handlers/admin_handler.go:45 declares 'IsAdmin bool `json:"is_admin" binding:"required"`'. go-playground/validator's required tag on a bool field rejects the zero value (false), so POST /admin/users/:email/admin with {"is_admin":false} always returns 400 'The request body could not be parsed' before ever reaching SetUserAdmin/SetUserAdminAtomic. Discovered while writing handler tests for gr-ax7 (a self-demotion test sending is_admin:false failed with the validator error instead of reaching the ErrSelfDemotion path). Net effect: the HTTP admin API can grant admin but can never revoke it -- the self-demotion guard in SetUserAdminAtomic is unreachable from this endpoint. Fix: drop binding:"required" from the IsAdmin field (bind is_admin as a plain optional bool; the field's presence isn't meaningful for a bool toggle) or switch to *bool if presence must be distinguished from false.
close_reason: Removed binding:"required" from IsAdmin bool field in admin_handler.go — the validator rejected the false zero-value, so revocation requests (is_admin:false) never reached SetUserAdmin/SetUserAdminAtomic. Added handler tests covering self-demotion (403) and successful revocation of another user's admin status (200).

title: Add concurrency guards to deploy-staging/deploy-prod/rollback workflows to prevent back-to-back-commit races
description:
None of .github/workflows/deploy-staging.yml, deploy-prod.yml, or rollback.yml declare a `concurrency:` group, so overlapping runs are possible and unhandled.

Concrete failure mode (deploy-staging.yml): it triggers on `workflow_run` completion of the Tests workflow on main, so two back-to-back commits produce two independent, unordered deploy-staging runs. Each run's "Delete stale staging versions" step computes KEEP_VERSION from its own head_sha and deletes every other staging-* version with traffic_split=0. If the run for an OLDER commit finishes after the run for a NEWER commit (e.g. slower Tests job), it deletes the newer commit's staging-<sha> version, silently reverting the staging environment. The two runs also both overwrite the same fixed 'staging' version concurrently, and may race at the App Engine deploy API level for the same service.

deploy-prod.yml and rollback.yml are lower probability (manual workflow_dispatch) but both call `gcloud app versions migrate` against the same production service's traffic split — if triggered close together (e.g. a rollback fired while a promotion is mid-flight) they could race for which version ends up serving 100% of traffic.

Fix: add `concurrency:` blocks:
- deploy-staging.yml: own group (e.g. `deploy-staging`), cancel-in-progress: false — queue rather than cancel, so cleanup always runs after the true latest deploy has completed, and no gcloud deploy is killed mid-flight leaving a half-created version.
- deploy-prod.yml and rollback.yml: share one group (e.g. `production-deploy`), cancel-in-progress: false — they both mutate the same production traffic split and must never run concurrently with each other.

Document the reasoning as a comment in the workflow files (why cancel-in-progress is false, why prod/rollback share a group) since this is non-obvious from the YAML alone.
design: Serialize via GitHub Actions concurrency groups rather than app-level locking. cancel-in-progress:false chosen over true because killing a gcloud app deploy mid-flight can leave a version in a broken/partial state — queuing costs a little CI time but preserves correctness. deploy-prod and rollback share a group because both mutate production traffic split (gcloud app versions migrate) and racing there is a production incident, not just a staging inconvenience.
acceptance_criteria:
- deploy-staging.yml has a concurrency group so two back-to-back commits' deploys serialize instead of racing on the stale-version cleanup step
- deploy-prod.yml and rollback.yml share a concurrency group so a promotion and a rollback can never run concurrently
- All three use cancel-in-progress: false
- docs/deployment.md updated to note the concurrency behavior
close_reason: Added concurrency: groups to deploy-staging.yml (own group), and shared production-deploy group across deploy-prod.yml + rollback.yml, all cancel-in-progress: false. Documented in docs/deployment.md.

title: Per-feed GET /api/feeds/:id/articles has no cursor pagination — returns full list
description:
While fixing gr-1s4 (stale pagination test skip), discovered that cursor-based pagination is only implemented on the 'all articles' endpoint. In internal/handlers/feed_handler.go GetArticles(), the branch for a specific feed ID (idStr != "all") calls fh.feedService.GetUserFeedArticles(user.ID, id), which returns every article for that feed as a plain array — it ignores limit/cursor/unread_only query params entirely and never returns next_cursor.

This matters because both native mobile epics assume this endpoint already paginates:
- gr-oksi.9 (Android): 'Cursor-based pagination via Paging 3 library, using next_cursor from /api/feeds/:id/articles'
- gr-u32y (iOS): 'Cursor-based pagination ... using next_cursor from /api/feeds/:id/articles'

For a feed with hundreds/thousands of articles, the mobile clients would either break (expecting a next_cursor field that's never present) or silently fetch everything in one response.

Fix: extend GetArticles' non-'all' branch to reuse the same cursor-pagination path as the 'all' branch (GetUserArticlesPaginated already accepts an optional feed filter, or add one), returning {"articles": [...], "next_cursor": "..."} for a single feed just like the 'all' endpoint does. Update the web frontend's per-feed article fetch to handle the new response shape (currently expects a bare array) in the same commit.

Blocks gr-oksi.9 and gr-u32y — mobile article-list work should not proceed against the current unpaginated per-feed endpoint.
close_reason: Added GetUserFeedArticlesPaginated to the Database interface (SQLite + Datastore), refactored GetArticles' per-feed branch to use it (same {articles, next_cursor} response shape as the 'all' endpoint), and updated the frontend to send limit/cursor/unread_only for single-feed fetches and enable Load More / server-side unread filtering there too. Added DB-layer, handler, and integration test coverage. Unblocks gr-oksi.9 and gr-u32y.

title: Auto-rollback production on failed smoke check or post-deploy health regression
description:
gr-euu promotes with 'gcloud app versions migrate' which shifts 100% of traffic immediately. gr-85d's smoke check runs after that and only fails the CI job — it does not undo the traffic shift, so a broken version stays fully live until a human notices the red workflow and manually runs gr-v2x. This is the exact scenario the epic exists to prevent (a bad deploy spiking latency or cost with nobody watching).

What to build:
1. If the post-deploy smoke check (gr-85d) fails, automatically invoke the rollback workflow (gr-v2x) to migrate traffic back to the previously-live version, then fail the job loudly (not silently).
2. Add a bounded post-promote health watch: after promotion, poll Cloud Monitoring for ~10-15 minutes for the same signals already defined in monitoring/alert-policies.yaml (5xx rate, p95 latency, instance count, Datastore read/write rate, egress). If any threshold is breached during the window, auto-invoke gr-v2x and notify.
3. Document the auto-rollback triggers and the health-watch window in docs/deployment.md in the same commit (per the epic's documentation policy).

Depends on gr-euu (promote workflow), gr-85d (smoke check), and gr-v2x (rollback workflow) existing first, since this wires the three together.
close_reason: Closed

title: Debug and fix iPad landscape Safari chrome overlap — systematic approach
description:
Three commits (224c374, 20b75d1, aef13cf) have attempted to fix navigation buttons being hidden behind Safari chrome on iPad landscape without success. All fixes so far have been CSS guesses without confirming the actual root cause on device.

**What we know**
- iPad landscape is typically ≥1024px wide, so it falls into the **desktop CSS path** (`min-width: 1024px`) — not the tablet or mobile path.
- All safe-area-inset rules and `100dvh`/`-webkit-fill-available` height fixes live inside `max-width: 1023px`, so **none of them apply on iPad landscape**.
- `body` globally has `height: 100dvh` (lines 38-39 in styles.css), but `#app` does not get `100dvh` or `-webkit-fill-available` at ≥1024px.
- `viewport-fit=cover` was removed in 20b75d1, which means `env(safe-area-inset-*)` returns 0 everywhere — making any safe-area padding rules inert.

**What needs to happen before writing more CSS**

1. **Use Safari Remote Web Inspector** — connect iPad via USB, open Safari > Develop > [device] > GoRead. Confirm exactly which element is being obscured and by how many pixels. Screenshot the computed styles panel for `body`, `#app`, `.header`, and `.main-container` in landscape.

2. **Check actual CSS breakpoint hit** — in the Web Inspector console, run: `window.innerWidth` and `document.documentElement.clientWidth` to confirm the device is ≥1024px (desktop path) vs 1023px (tablet path). Some iPad models in landscape may be exactly 1024px.

3. **Determine the exact failure mode** — is the header clipped by the top toolbar, or do bottom nav controls fall below the bottom edge? Or is it a scroll container that can scroll past visible content?

4. **Check if `-webkit-fill-available` on body or `#app` would solve it** — try `document.querySelector('#app').style.height = '-webkit-fill-available'` in the console to see if it fixes the layout live.

5. **Fix**: Based on findings, likely need to add to the `min-width: 1024px` block (or globally):
   - `#app { height: 100dvh; }` (currently missing at desktop)
   - Possibly `padding-top: env(safe-area-inset-top)` on `#app` — but only works if `viewport-fit=cover` is present; confirm if re-adding it is needed and safe.
design: Do not write more CSS until Safari Remote Web Inspector confirms the exact element and pixel offset being obscured. Previous attempts failed because we were guessing at the cause. The breakpoint mismatch (all fixes in max-width:1023px but iPad landscape hits ≥1024px) is a strong lead — verify this first.
acceptance_criteria:
- Nav buttons and header are fully visible in iPad landscape with Safari toolbar visible
- No regression on desktop (macOS Safari/Chrome), iPhone portrait, iPhone landscape, iPad portrait
- Fix is grounded in confirmed Web Inspector findings, not another CSS guess
close_reason: Fix shipped in 52e6aae (2026-06-28): added #app { display:flex; flex-direction:column; height:100dvh; } globally, resolving the min-width:1024px breakpoint mismatch this issue identified as root cause. Status sync was dropped after the fix landed (same pattern as gr-7ifn).

title: CI/CD pipeline: staging environment, approval gate, and test coverage
description:
Epic tracking all work to replace manual 'make deploy-prod' with a proper CI/CD pipeline. Covers: automated CI hardening, secret management cleanup, GCP Workload Identity Federation, staging deploys, human approval gate for production, post-deploy smoke checks, systematic test coverage improvements, and post-deploy safety nets (auto-rollback, alerting, billing budget).

Work is sequenced with hard dependencies — issues unlock in phases as prerequisites close. Do not start any issue without checking its dependencies are closed first.

Scope: Go/App Engine backend pipeline only. Native app (iOS/Android) build and store-release pipelines are tracked separately as gr-ezwq and gr-i90t under their own mobile epics (gr-43g, gr-oksi) — see gr-87j for the scoping decision.

Documentation policy: every phase task that changes build system, CI, tests, or deployment config must update the relevant docs/*.md (docs/testing.md, docs/deployment.md, docs/monitoring.md) in the same commit — see gr-87j for details. Do not defer doc updates to the cutover task (gr-wnb5).

Post-deploy safety net (added after review — a promote should never be able to silently spike cost or kill latency): gr-b6ko auto-rolls-back production on a failed smoke check or a breached health-signal window, blocked on gr-euu/gr-85d/gr-v2x/gr-edpp existing first. gr-edpp wires real notification channels into the already-written but never-connected monitoring/alert-policies.yaml (all 6 policies currently have notificationChannels: [] — they'd fire into the void). gr-86j3 adds a Cloud Billing Budget as a hard dollar-based backstop, since Monitoring API cannot see actual billing.googleapis.com/cost metrics — the existing 6 policies are cost-correlated proxies only, not real thresholds. Note app.yaml already caps max_instances: 10, bounding compute run-away independent of these.
close_reason: All phases complete: CI hardening, secret management, WIF auth, staging/prod deploy workflows with human approval gate, post-deploy smoke checks + auto-rollback + alerting + billing budget safety net, systematic test coverage improvements (handlers, admin CLI, feed fixtures, security regression suite, benchmarks), the manual make deploy-dev/deploy-prod cutover (gr-wnb5), and the Datastore emulator in CI (gr-m31, verified end-to-end on live CI runs). Follow-up gr-4avl (writing actual DatastoreDB interface tests against the now-available emulator) filed as a standalone backlog task rather than under this epic, since it's incremental coverage work rather than pipeline infrastructure.

title: [bug] Article content scrolls behind fixed bottom nav bar on phone portrait
description:
On phone portrait, .content-pane has height: 100% but .main-container accounts for the bottom nav with margin-bottom. The content pane itself has no bottom padding, so the last ~64px of a long article is permanently hidden behind the fixed bottom nav bar (z-index 1000).

Fix: add padding-bottom: calc(64px + env(safe-area-inset-bottom, 0px)) to .content-pane inside the phone portrait media query (styles.css ~line 2060).

File: web/static/css/styles.css
close_reason: Closed

title: [bug] Phone portrait pane switching broken on iOS 15 (CSS :has() unsupported)
description:
The sidebar-wrapper slide animation uses CSS :has() (styles.css:2043-2046) as the sole mechanism to show/hide the feeds and articles panes on phone portrait. JS correctly toggles .active on child panes, but the parent transform is CSS-only. iOS 15 and below (Safari < 15.4) do not support :has(), so the Feeds and Articles panes are permanently inaccessible on those devices.

Fix: in setupMobileNavigation() and updateMobileNavigation() in app.js, set sidebar-wrapper's transform directly in JS instead of relying on :has(). Remove the :has() rules from styles.css.

Files: web/static/js/app.js (setupMobileNavigation ~line 586, updateMobileNavigation ~line 644), web/static/css/styles.css (lines 2043-2046)
close_reason: Closed

title: Accessibility: static ARIA labels, modal roles, Esc key, contrast fix
description:
Split from gr-68. Low-risk, no dynamic-content changes.

Work items:
1. Add aria-label to the ~14 interactive elements in index.html that lack one (icon buttons, close buttons, filter controls)
2. Add role="dialog", aria-modal="true", aria-labelledby to the three modals in index.html and the inline modal built in app.js
3. Wire Esc key to close modals in modals.js (keydown handler already exists in app.js at line 750; extend it)
4. Remove opacity: 0.6 from .article-item.read (styles.css:538) — use color-only dimming instead to pass WCAG AA contrast
5. Add a visually-hidden skip-to-content link at the top of index.html

Files: web/templates/index.html, web/static/css/styles.css, web/static/js/modals.js

Acceptance criteria:
- All icon/action buttons have descriptive aria-label values
- All three modals have role=dialog, aria-modal=true, aria-labelledby pointing at their heading
- Pressing Esc closes whichever modal is open
- Read articles are visually dimmed without opacity (use color values only)
- A skip-to-content link is present and focusable by keyboard
design: Drop opacity:0.6 entirely; the title/meta already switch to muted colors (#5f6368, #80868b) so contrast is maintained. Esc key wiring goes in the existing keydown handler in app.js rather than in modals.js to keep all keyboard shortcuts in one place.
notes: Deliberately excludes focus trapping and aria-live regions — those are tracked in the companion issue. Modals already lazy-load via modals.js so Esc wiring should call the existing hideXxxModal() methods.
close_reason: All five items complete: static aria-labels, modal roles, Esc key, contrast fix (opacity removed), skip-to-content link

title: Fix errcheck linter failure on tx.Rollback in schema.go
description: The golangci-lint errcheck rule flags the unhandled return value of tx.Rollback() in SetUserAdminAtomic. This breaks the CI/CD lint job. Fix by wrapping the defer in an anonymous function that discards the error explicitly.
close_reason: Closed

title: Window unread count queries to 90 days to reduce Firestore reads
description: getFeedUnreadCountForUser scans ALL articles ever stored in a feed. For a feed with 500 articles, each cache miss costs 500+ reads per feed. Adding a 90-day published_at filter matches the existing FilterExistingArticleURLs pattern and dramatically reduces reads for long-running feeds.
close_reason: Implemented 90-day published_at filter on getFeedUnreadCountForUser in both Datastore and SQLite, plus GetAccountStats SQLite query. All tests pass.

title: Use UpdateCount instead of Invalidate in MarkUserArticleRead
description:
feed_service.go:389-390 — MarkUserArticleRead calls unreadCache.Invalidate(userID) after every mark-read action. This throws away the entire unread count cache for the user, so the very next page load triggers GetUserUnreadCounts → N+1 Datastore query pattern (one goroutine per feed, each doing a keys-only query + GetMulti chunk reads).

unread_cache.go:87 has UpdateCount(userID, feedID, wasRead, nowRead) specifically designed for incremental cache updates, but it is never called from the service layer.

Fix: Extend the MarkRead API request body to include feedID and wasRead (the previous read state). The client already has both values — it is displaying the article inside a specific feed. Then call UpdateCount instead of Invalidate.

This converts a cache-busting O(N*M) Datastore operation into an O(1) in-memory map update on every article read action. Given that reading articles is the primary user action, this is the highest-frequency optimization available.
close_reason: Implemented: MarkUserArticleRead now calls UpdateCount(userID, feedID, wasRead, isRead) when feedID>0, falling back to Invalidate only when feedID is unknown. Handler extended with feed_id/was_read fields; JS client passes both values at every call site.

title: Add global request body size limit middleware
description: Only OPML import has a file size check. Other API endpoints accept unlimited JSON payloads, enabling memory exhaustion attacks. Add http.MaxBytesReader middleware globally with a 1MB default limit. File: main.go.
close_reason: Added RequestBodyLimit middleware in internal/middleware/body_limit.go with 1MB global default and 10MB override for OPML import. Wired into main.go after SecurityHeaders. 6 tests added, all passing.

title: Add server-side HTML sanitization for feed content
description: Article content is stored raw from RSS feeds. XSS prevention relies entirely on client-side DOMPurify. If DOMPurify is bypassed or fails to load, stored XSS is possible. Add server-side sanitizer like bluemonday for defense-in-depth. File: internal/services/feed_service.go around lines 591-605.
close_reason: Added bluemonday UGCPolicy sanitization to Description and Content fields in all three feed converters (RSS, RDF, Atom). Policy initialized once on FeedService construction and applied via sanitizeHTML helper.

title: Add Subresource Integrity (SRI) to DOMPurify CDN script
description: index.html line 240 loads DOMPurify from jsdelivr without an integrity attribute. A CDN compromise would inject malicious code directly into the sanitization library. Add integrity=sha384-... and crossorigin=anonymous attributes. File: web/templates/index.html line 240.
close_reason: Added integrity=sha384-... and crossorigin=anonymous to DOMPurify CDN script tag in index.html line 237.

title: Fix broken image lazy loading (CSS vs HTML attribute)
description: styles.css:928 sets loading: lazy as a CSS property, but loading is an HTML attribute and has no effect in CSS. Images currently all load eagerly, wasting bandwidth especially on mobile. Need to inject loading=lazy decoding=async as attributes on img tags in the JS rendering code. File: web/static/css/styles.css line 928, web/static/js/app.js line 1507.
close_reason: Removed invalid CSS loading:lazy rule; now sets loading=lazy and decoding=async as DOM attributes on img elements after content render

title: Return article previews (no content) in list/pagination APIs
description: GetUserArticlesPaginated() and GetUserFeedArticles() fetch the full Content field for every article even though the frontend only uses description in list view. Create a separate list query or projection that omits Content to cut payload sizes dramatically. Files: internal/database/datastore.go, internal/handlers/feed_handler.go, internal/handlers/article_handler.go.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add HTTP conditional requests (ETag/If-Modified-Since) to feed fetching
description: Every hourly feed refresh downloads full content even if unchanged. Store ETag/Last-Modified from responses and send them on subsequent requests. Handle 304 Not Modified responses to skip parsing. Estimated ~90% bandwidth reduction for feed fetching. Files: internal/services/feed_service.go (fetchFeed around line 509-527), feed entity needs new fields for etag/last_modified.
close_reason: Implemented HTTP conditional requests (ETag/If-Modified-Since) for feed fetching. Added FetchOptions, 304 handling, response header capture, cache header persistence, schema migration, and comprehensive tests.

title: Fix data race in TestSessionExpiration mockDB
description:
The async DeleteSession change (gr-5iz) introduced a data race in TestSessionExpiration.

Problem: mockDB in session_test.go doesn't have mutex protection for its sessions map. When the async DeleteSession goroutine writes to the map while the test reads from it, we get a race detected by -race flag.

Location: internal/auth/session_test.go

Fix needed:
1. Add sync.RWMutex to mockDB struct
2. Add locking in GetSession, CreateSession, DeleteSession, and DeleteExpiredSessions methods

The race is between:
- mockDB.GetSession() reading m.sessions (line 82)
- mockDB.DeleteSession() deleting from m.sessions (line 96)
close_reason: Added mutex protection to mockDB in session_test.go to fix data race

Improve accessibility throughout the application with proper ARIA labels, keyboard navigation, and screen reader support.

title: subscription_service.go:ValidateAdminToken ignores timestamp update errors
description: In ValidateAdminToken (lines 280-286 for SQLite, 308-316 for Datastore), the last_used_at timestamp update errors are logged but don't fail validation. If the update fails, token usage tracking becomes unreliable. Error: 'Warning: Failed to update last_used_at' is printed but validation still succeeds. Fix: Return error if timestamp update fails, or at minimum track failed updates for monitoring.
close_reason: Return (true, err) from ValidateAdminToken when last_used_at update fails; update admin CLI caller to log warning and continue rather than exit on valid-token tracking failure

title: HTML tag stripper is vulnerable to malformed HTML
description:
stripHTMLTags uses simple character-by-character parsing. Malformed HTML with unclosed tags could leave inTag=true and strip all content.

Location: internal/services/feed_service.go:1200-1226
Impact: Data loss, articles with no content if malformed HTML

Fix: Use proper HTML parser like golang.org/x/net/html
close_reason: stripHTMLTags already rewritten to use golang.org/x/net/html parser in feed_service.go:1338

title: Hardcoded site-specific feed URLs
description:
addFeedForUserInternal has hardcoded feed URLs for specific sites (slashdot, nytimes, seattletimes). Unmaintainable and breaks if sites change feeds.

Location: internal/services/feed_service.go:234-240
Impact: Brittle, requires code changes when sites change feeds

Fix: Use feed discovery for all sites or externalize to configuration
close_reason: Closed

title: No validation of fetched secrets
description:
GetSecret returns raw secret data without validating it's non-empty or has expected format. Could return empty secrets.

Location: internal/secrets/secrets.go:37, 53, 65
Impact: Silent failures with empty secrets, cryptic errors later

Fix: Validate secrets are non-empty and match expected format (e.g., Stripe keys have prefix)
close_reason: Added non-empty validation in GetOAuthCredentials and GetStripeCredentials. 6 new tests added covering each credential being empty.

title: Weak local cron authentication
description:
Local cron endpoints only check user.IsAdmin without requiring ADMIN_TOKEN. Any admin user can trigger expensive operations.

Location: internal/handlers/feed_handler.go:288-294, 332-337
Impact: Privileged users can trigger expensive cron operations

Fix: Require ADMIN_TOKEN header even for authenticated admin users in local env
close_reason: Local cron endpoints now require admin session + matching X-Admin-Token header. Covers RefreshFeeds, CleanupOrphanedUserArticles, CleanupExpiredSessions. 12 new/updated tests, all passing.

title: Missing rate limiting on Stripe webhook
description:
Stripe webhook endpoint has no rate limiting. Attacker could flood with fake webhook requests.

Location: internal/handlers/payment_handler.go:68-189
Impact: DoS via webhook flooding, log spam

Fix: Add rate limiting middleware specifically for webhook endpoint
close_reason: Added webhookRateLimiter (5 req/s, burst 10) in main.go and applied auth.RateLimitMiddleware to the /webhooks/stripe route.

title: Missing Datastore transaction support
description:
Datastore implementation has no transaction wrapper for multi-entity operations. BatchSetUserArticleStatus can fail partially with no rollback.

Location: internal/database/datastore.go
Impact: Data integrity risk on partial failures

Fix: Implement transaction wrapper using Datastore transactions or implement compensating rollback
close_reason: Wrapped BatchSetUserArticleStatus chunk loop in RunInTransaction so any chunk failure rolls back the entire operation. All existing tests pass.

title: Silent failure on duplicate article insert
description:
AddArticle uses INSERT OR IGNORE which silently fails on duplicate URL. Returns LAST_INSERT_ID which may be 0 for ignored inserts.

Location: internal/database/schema.go:470-491
Impact: Could lead to incorrect article ID assignment

Fix: Use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE or explicitly check for duplicates first
close_reason: Closed during rebase session — see prior commit history for details

title: Standardize getting-started/clone instructions across README, setup.md, CONTRIBUTING
description: README.md, docs/setup.md, and CONTRIBUTING.md each give different prerequisites, env-var setup, and run instructions for local dev. Notably CONTRIBUTING.md tells contributors to 'cp .env.example .env' but the app never reads .env files (no dotenv loading in code), so that step does nothing useful. Align prerequisites (Node/npm optional-for-frontend framing), env var setup, and recommended run command (make dev) across all three docs.
close_reason: Aligned prerequisites, env-var setup, and run/test commands across README.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, and docs/setup.md; fixed CONTRIBUTING.md's misleading .env.example copy step

title: Standardize documentation IA and format across docs/
description: Apply a consistent template (TOC, heading levels, Overview/Config/Testing/Troubleshooting/Related order) to every file in docs/. Consolidate duplicated inline troubleshooting sections into troubleshooting.md with pointers. Fix factual errors found along the way: Pro subscription price documented as $2.99/month in 5 places but Stripe UnitAmount is 999 ($9.99); stripe.md documents nonexistent cmd/setup-stripe subcommands and nonexistent logs/goread2.log; troubleshooting.md links to API.md (wrong case, file is api.md); deployment.md documents an untested Docker/VPS self-host path with no Dockerfile in repo, being removed per user decision to keep docs matching the one real, CI'd deployment path (App Engine).
close_reason: Applied a consistent template (TOC, Related Documentation, standard section order) to every doc in docs/. Consolidated duplicated inline troubleshooting sections into troubleshooting.md with pointers from each doc. Fixed the $2.99->$9.99 price error (6 occurrences), the API.md->api.md broken link, nonexistent stripe CLI subcommands and logs/goread2.log references, and removed the untested Docker/VPS self-host path from deployment.md/troubleshooting.md per user decision. Also stripped a 230-line historical changelog from security.md and a stale roadmap/TODO section from testing.md, per CLAUDE.md's docs-describe-current-state rule.

title: rollback.yml capture/cleanup steps use stale prod-* filter
description: rollback.yml's 'Capture currently-live version before migrating' and 'Delete stale production versions' steps filter on version.id:prod-*, but promoted versions have kept their staging-<sha> name since the 2026-07-05 naming decision (see deployment.md). The migrate step itself still works since it targets an explicit input version, but the capture-previous and cleanup logic are currently permanent no-ops for any post-2026-07-05 rollback. Found while auditing docs/deployment.md against .github/workflows/rollback.yml.
close_reason: Fixed capture-live-version filter to use traffic_split=1.0 (no name pattern); scoped cleanup to legacy prod-* naming with explanatory comment, matching the already-correct deploy-prod.yml pattern. Updated docs/deployment.md to remove the now-stale Known gap note.

title: Write DatastoreDB interface tests against the CI emulator (GetUserFeedArticles, MarkUserArticleRead, etc.)
description: gr-m31 wired a Cloud Datastore emulator into CI (test.yml) so DATASTORE_EMULATOR_HOST-gated tests can run for real instead of skipping. But the only existing emulator-gated test file, internal/services/admin_token_datastore_test.go, only exercises AdminToken/User entities via the raw *datastore.Client from DatastoreDB.GetClient() (used directly by SubscriptionService) -- it never calls through DatastoreDB's own methods that implement the database.Database interface. Verified via 'go test -coverpkg=./internal/database/... ./internal/services/...' with the emulator running: only NewDatastoreDB/GetClient/Close show any coverage; AddFeed, UpdateFeed, GetFeeds, AddArticle, GetArticles, CreateUser, GetUserFeeds, SubscribeUserToFeed, GetUserFeedArticles, MarkUserArticleRead, ToggleUserArticleStar, GetUserUnreadCounts, GetAccountStats, CleanupOrphanedUserArticles, and ~20 others in internal/database/datastore.go all remain at 0%. Fix: write a test suite (e.g. internal/database/datastore_test.go, gated the same way as admin_token_datastore_test.go on DATASTORE_EMULATOR_HOST) that exercises DatastoreDB directly through the Database interface, mirroring the existing SQLite schema_test.go coverage. Note --consistency=1.0 is required when starting the emulator for these tests to be non-flaky (see docs/testing.md's Datastore Emulator section for why).
close_reason: Added internal/database/datastore_test.go and datastore_user_article_test.go: ~40 emulator-gated tests exercising DatastoreDB through the database.Database interface (feeds, articles, users, subscriptions/admin, sessions, audit logs). Coverage of internal/database goes from ~30 methods at 0% to 83.7% overall when run with DATASTORE_EMULATOR_HOST set. Also fixed a real bug found along the way (gr-7ifn): UpdateSessionExpiry used the untagged Session struct instead of SessionEntity and always failed to load sessions written by CreateSession. docs/testing.md updated.

title: Manual: register staging OAuth redirect URI + verify staging login round-trip
description: gr-furl implemented host-aware OAuth redirect URL selection (internal/auth/auth.go) and deploy-staging.yml now deploys a fixed 'staging' App Engine version, but two things require a human and haven't happened yet: (1) add https://staging-dot-goread-467200.uc.r.appspot.com/auth/callback as an additional Authorized redirect URI on the existing Google OAuth client in GCP Console (APIs & Services > Credentials) — this is a manual console step, not automatable; (2) after the next merge to main deploys the new 'staging' version, verify a full Google login round-trip on https://staging-dot-goread-467200.uc.r.appspot.com and confirm production login still works unaffected. Closes out gr-furl's acceptance criteria, which requires a live verified login round-trip, not just the code change.

title: Auto-cleanup stale App Engine versions in deploy-staging and rollback workflows
description:
Confirmed live: 4 staging-* versions had already accumulated in App Engine after only 2 days of deploy-staging.yml running (one per push to main), with no cleanup anywhere. Policy requested: always have exactly (1) the current production version, (2) the last-known-good production version (for fast rollback), and (3) at most one staging version at any time — everything else should be deleted automatically, not left to accumulate or rely on a time-based sweep.

What to build:
1. deploy-staging.yml: after a successful deploy, delete every other staging-* version, keeping only the one just deployed.
2. rollback.yml: after a successful migrate, keep only the 2 most-recently-created prod-* versions (current + previous), delete the rest.
3. Update gr-euu's description: its own cleanup job (deploy-prod.yml, not yet built) currently says 'delete zero-traffic versions older than 7 days, keep last 3' — replace with the same 'keep current + previous only' policy for consistency once it's built.
4. One-time manual cleanup of the versions that already accumulated.
close_reason:
Added cleanup logic to both workflows that create/change App Engine versions:

deploy-staging.yml: after a successful deploy, deletes every other staging-* version, keeping only the one just deployed. Verified live — 5 stale staging versions (staging-06e649a, staging-2018fef, staging-23511d8, staging-8333ae0, staging-905af52) that had accumulated over the session were all correctly identified and deleted in a single run, leaving only staging-66379a3. Staging URL confirmed HTTP 200 afterward.

rollback.yml: captures whichever prod-* version currently has 100% traffic BEFORE migrating, then after migrating deletes every prod-* version except the new current one and that captured previous one. Deliberately not "keep 2 most recent by creation time" — that would incorrectly delete the version just rolled back to if someone rolls back more than one step (since it wouldn't be among the 2 newest by creation time). Not live-tested against a real multi-step rollback scenario (no reason to exercise that path against production), but the logic and gcloud filter syntax were validated locally.

Updated gr-euu's description so its own future cleanup job (deploy-prod.yml, not yet built) uses the same precise "current + previous only" policy instead of its previous "keep last 3 within 7 days" — a looser rule that could leave clutter or delete a version still needed depending on deploy cadence.

Current state: exactly 1 staging version + 2 prod versions (current + previous), matching the requested invariant exactly.

title: Resolve govulncheck findings: Go 1.25 toolchain + golang.org/x/net v0.55.0
description:
First real run of the govulncheck CI job (gr-eib) surfaced 3 vulnerabilities, all fixed by dependency/toolchain bumps rather than code changes:

1. GO-2026-5039 (net/textproto, stdlib): arbitrary inputs included in errors without escaping. Fixed in go1.25.11. Reached via internal/handlers/feed_handler.go:549 (ImportOPML -> io.ReadAll -> textproto.Reader.ReadMIMEHeader).
2. GO-2026-5037 (crypto/x509, stdlib): inefficient candidate hostname parsing. Fixed in go1.25.11. Reached via internal/services/feed_discovery.go:421 (isValidFeed -> x509 cert verification) and internal/handlers/admin_handler.go:136.
3. GO-2026-5030 (golang.org/x/net, XSS via duplicate attributes in html.Parse). Fixed in golang.org/x/net v0.55.0 (currently v0.41.0). Reached via internal/services/feed_service.go:1394 (stripHTMLTags -> html.Parse).

Fix: bump go.mod toolchain to 1.25.x (verify compatibility with existing tests/deps first) and run 'go get golang.org/x/net@v0.55.0' (or later). Re-run govulncheck to confirm all 3 clear. Low urgency — these require an attacker to control feed content already reachable in specific admin/import paths, not directly internet-facing without authentication, but should still be cleared since a fix is available with no code changes.
close_reason:
Bumped go.mod (go 1.25.0 / toolchain go1.25.11) and golang.org/x/net to v0.55.0, clearing all 3 govulncheck findings from the first real CI run (GO-2026-5039, GO-2026-5037, GO-2026-5030). Verified locally with govulncheck: 0 vulnerabilities affecting our code. Updated app.yaml's App Engine runtime to go125, verified live with a throwaway deploy before committing (HTTP 200, then deleted). Updated all Go-version references across CI, README, and docs.

Hit two follow-on CI breakages while verifying this in the real pipeline (not just locally) and fixed both:
1. golangci-lint-action's "version: latest" resolved to v1.64.8 (built with go1.24), which refuses to lint code targeting go1.25.11 ("Go language version used to build golangci-lint is lower than the targeted Go version"). Pinned to v2.12.2 explicitly.
2. That alone wasn't enough: golangci-lint-action@v6 itself hard-refuses golangci-lint v2 ("you must update to golangci-lint-action v7"). Bumped the action to v9.3.0 (current latest, not just the bare-minimum v7), pinned to commit SHA.

Final verification: the real Tests + deploy-staging pipeline ran end-to-end successfully — govulncheck job now reports success on its own (not just continue-on-error masking failures), lint passes, and staging-4f0edc6 deployed with go125 runtime, auto-cleaned to be the only staging version, confirmed HTTP 200. (Note: GitHub's own Actions run-status API showed stale/inconsistent in_progress status for a while after the deploy had actually already completed and the version existed in GCP — verified against GCP ground truth directly rather than trusting the lagging API.)

title: Migrate Stripe secrets (4 vars) to runtime Secret Manager fetch, same as OAuth/CSRF/admin
description:
gr-0tx moved CSRF_SECRET, ADMIN_TOKEN, and INITIAL_ADMIN_EMAILS off the disk-based substitute-secrets flow, following the pattern already used for GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID/SECRET (absent from app.yaml, fetched via secrets.GetXCredentials() at runtime with a hardcoded Secret Manager name). The four Stripe variables (STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY, STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET, STRIPE_PRICE_ID) still use the old pattern: app.yaml has ${STRIPE_*} placeholders, and 'make substitute-secrets' shells out to gcloud, puts the real values in shell env vars, and writes them into app-deploy.yaml on disk before 'gcloud app deploy' — the exact secret-exposure risk gr-0tx was created to eliminate.

Complication found while scoping gr-0tx: internal/services/payment_service.go and internal/config/validation.go already call secrets.GetStripeCredentials(ctx), which checks os.Getenv("STRIPE_SECRET_KEY") first — but since substitute-secrets currently populates that env var with the REAL secret value at deploy time, GetStripeCredentials never actually reaches Secret Manager in production today; it just consumes the value already written to disk by envsubst. Simply deleting the STRIPE_* app.yaml lines without any other change should be safe, since an absent env var reads as empty string, triggering the Secret Manager fetch, matching the OAuth pattern exactly — but verify this explicitly before deploying.

What to do:
1. Remove the four STRIPE_* placeholder lines from app.yaml
2. Remove the four STRIPE_* lines from the Makefile's substitute-secrets target
3. Once nothing substitutes secrets anymore, delete the substitute-secrets target entirely and its dependency from deploy-dev/deploy-prod (this is also a prerequisite noted in gr-wnb5's cutover task)
4. Update docs/deployment.md and docs/STRIPE.md to reflect Stripe secrets are runtime-fetched, not deploy-time substituted

Discovered while working gr-0tx.
close_reason:
Completed as a direct fix while debugging gr-rfd's first staging deploy, which 503'd with "Configuration validation failed: Stripe: STRIPE_SECRET_KEY does not start with 'sk_'" — deploy-staging.yml deploys app.yaml directly with no envsubst step, so the ${STRIPE_*} placeholders were being deployed as literal broken strings, exactly the risk this issue was filed to address.

Done:
1. Removed all four Stripe placeholders from app.yaml — verified the four secrets already exist in Secret Manager (stripe-secret-key, stripe-publishable-key, stripe-webhook-secret, stripe-price-id) and the App Engine service account already has secretAccessor. Manually deployed a throwaway version to confirm secrets.GetStripeCredentials() correctly falls through to Secret Manager (HTTP 200, correct fetch logged), then verified the real automated pipeline deploys cleanly too.
2. Simplified the Makefile's substitute-secrets target to a plain 'cp app.yaml app-deploy.yaml' — app.yaml now has zero ${VAR} placeholders left (Stripe was the last), so the gcloud secret-fetching in that target was pure dead weight.
3/4. Left substitute-secrets' full removal and the deploy-dev/deploy-prod Makefile targets to gr-wnb5 as originally scoped (updated gr-wnb5's description, which had a stale assumption that gr-0tx already fully removed substitute-secrets). docs/STRIPE.md had no stale substitute-secrets references to update.

Verified with go test -race ./... and make lint (unaffected by these changes) plus the live deploy verification described above.

title: Run frontend Jest tests in CI — currently only local via make test
description: While fixing gr-eib (CI workflow), noticed test.sh runs 'npm test' (140 Jest tests across 7 suites) locally as part of 'make test', but .github/workflows/test.yml never runs them — CI only verifies the frontend builds (gr-eib's frontend-build job), not that its tests pass. A JS regression that breaks a Jest suite would merge clean today. Fix: add a step (or job) running 'npm run test:ci' (already defined in package.json) in CI, alongside or inside the frontend-build job. Related to gr-il9c (ESLint static analysis) which covers a different gap — that one is lint, this one is the existing test suite never being wired in.
close_reason: Added 'npm run test:ci' step to the frontend-build job in .github/workflows/test.yml, right after npm ci and before make build-frontend. Verified locally: all 140 Jest tests across 7 suites pass under test:ci mode. Updated docs/testing.md's CI/CD Integration section to reflect the new step.

title: Configure GCP Billing Budget alert for hard monthly spend cap
description:
docs/monitoring.md explicitly states billing.googleapis.com/cost/* metrics are not queryable via the Cloud Monitoring API, so the existing 6 alert policies (Datastore ops, egress, instance count, latency, 5xx rate) are cost-correlated proxies, not actual dollar thresholds. docs/performance.md already recommends budget alerts at 50/80/100% of monthly budget but this was never implemented or tracked as work — it's just a paragraph in a doc.

What to build:
- A Cloud Billing Budget (via 'gcloud billing budgets create' or console — separate API/product from Cloud Monitoring) scoped to the goread-467200 project
- Thresholds at 50%, 80%, and 100% of expected monthly spend, each emailing the account owner
- Optional: Pub/Sub notification wired to a Cloud Function that pages via the same channel as the operational alerts (gr-<notification-channel-issue>), for a single unified alert path
- Document the configured budget amount and thresholds in docs/monitoring.md

This is a hard backstop independent of the operational proxy metrics — catches scenarios the proxies might miss (e.g. a pricing change, a non-Datastore/non-egress cost driver, or a proxy threshold set too loose).
close_reason:
Created a Cloud Billing Budget via gcloud (had to enable billingbudgets.googleapis.com first — was not previously enabled on the project): "GoRead2 Monthly Budget", $20/month, calendar-month period, scoped to projects/goread-467200 only, thresholds at 50/80/100%, notifying the same email channel created for gr-edpp (jeffreyp07@gmail.com) plus GCP's default IAM billing-admin recipients.

Discovered a pre-existing "$10 Monthly Budget Alert" (thresholds 50/90/100/150%) already on the billing account, not created by this work — flagged in docs/monitoring.md for Jeffrey to review/consolidate, did not touch it since removing someone else's existing config wasn't in scope.

Documented the new budget and how to adjust it in docs/monitoring.md under a new "Billing Budget" section. Verified via gcloud billing budgets list.

title: Wire real notification channels into monitoring/alert-policies.yaml
description:
All 6 alert policies in monitoring/alert-policies.yaml (high Datastore reads/writes, high instance count, high egress, entity read spike, high 5xx rate) have notificationChannels: [] — empty stubs. Even if these policies are deployed to GCP, they fire into the void; nobody gets paged.

What to do:
1. Confirm whether these policies were ever actually deployed via 'gcloud alpha monitoring policies create' (unclear from repo state alone — verify in GCP console).
2. Create real notification channels (email at minimum; Slack via monitoring/deploy-alerts.sh pattern already documented) with 'gcloud alpha monitoring channels create'.
3. Add the channel IDs to all 6 policies in alert-policies.yaml and (re)deploy.
4. Update docs/monitoring.md to remove the 'Add your notification channels here' placeholder language once real channels are wired.

This is a prerequisite for the auto-rollback health watch to be meaningful — an alert that fires but pages nobody is equivalent to no alert.
close_reason:
Created a real email notification channel (projects/goread-467200/notificationChannels/5854116738752263410, jeffreyp07@gmail.com) and wired it into all 6 alert policies via gcloud.

Along the way found the policies were only partially deployed: 4 of 6 existed in GCP (Datastore reads/writes, instance count, entity read spike), but "High Network Egress" and "High HTTP Error Rate" had never actually been created despite being in the repo's alert-policies.yaml. Deployed both now. The HTTP Error Rate policy's autoClose was set to 900s (15 min) in the YAML, which the Alerting API rejected — GCP enforces a 30-minute minimum. Fixed to 1800s in both the live policy and the source YAML.

Updated monitoring/alert-policies.yaml so all 6 notificationChannels arrays reflect the real deployed channel ID instead of empty placeholders, and updated docs/monitoring.md to describe current state, the one-policy-per-invocation deploy gotcha, and the autoClose minimum.

Verified: gcloud alpha monitoring policies list shows all 6 enabled with the channel attached; YAML parses cleanly and matches deployed state.

description:

title: Android: Article list screen with pagination and unread filter
description:
The article browser: paginated list of articles for a feed or 'All Articles', with unread filter toggle.

What to build:
- ArticleListScreen: LazyColumn of article rows (title, feed name, date, read/unread indicator)
- Cursor-based pagination via Paging 3 library, using next_cursor from /api/feeds/:id/articles
- Unread-only toggle (passes unread_only=true query param)
- Tap article → navigates to ArticleReaderScreen; marks article read immediately
- Pull-to-refresh resets cursor and reloads from page 1
- Empty state for feeds with no articles
- Works for both per-feed (/api/feeds/:id/articles) and all-articles (/api/feeds/all/articles) views

title: Android: NetworkClient — Retrofit/OkHttp, API models, error handling
description:
Build the shared networking layer all feature screens will use. Getting this right avoids N different ad-hoc OkHttp usages across the codebase.

What to build:
- Singleton NetworkClient wrapping Retrofit + OkHttp with a persistent CookieJar
- Suspend-function methods for each API endpoint: getFeeds, getArticles(feedId, cursor, unreadOnly), markRead, toggleStar, markAllRead, getUnreadCounts, getAccountStats, getSubscriptionInfo, importOpml, exportOpml, createPortalSession
- Kotlin data classes mirroring the JSON the existing handlers emit: Feed, Article, PaginatedArticles (articles + next_cursor), UnreadCounts, AccountStats, SubscriptionInfo
- Sealed NetworkError class (Unauthorized, NotFound, ServerError, NoConnection, DecodingError)
- CSRF token storage and automatic injection into mutating requests via X-CSRF-Token header (fetched from /api/me on login), via an OkHttp Interceptor

title: Android: Verify backend CORS and cookie compatibility with OkHttp
description:
Before building any screens, confirm the existing backend works correctly when called from OkHttp rather than a browser or iOS's URLSession. A failed assumption here would break auth or API calls for every screen downstream.

What to verify:
1. Session cookies: confirm OkHttp's CookieJar (backed by a persistent store, e.g. PersistentCookieJar) receives and replays the session cookie from /auth/callback correctly (SameSite=Lax should be fine with same-domain; cross-check against iOS findings in gr-n8ml)
2. CSRF: verify /api/me returns csrf_token and that the X-CSRF-Token header is accepted from OkHttp requests
3. CORS: check whether any CORS headers are needed for the OAuth redirect back to the app (Custom Tabs redirect vs App Links)
4. Document findings and any required backend changes in the issue notes before closing; note any divergence from the iOS findings (gr-n8ml)

title: Android: Google OAuth login via Custom Tabs
description:
Implement the full authentication flow so users can sign in with Google and stay signed in across app launches.

What to build:
- Call /auth/login to get the Google auth URL, then open it in a Custom Tab (androidx.browser)
- Handle the OAuth callback redirect (App Links or custom scheme intent-filter) to /auth/callback
- On success the session cookie is set — persist it via OkHttp's CookieJar backed by a durable store so it survives app restarts
- Call /api/me after login to confirm session and fetch CSRF token
- LoginScreen shown when no session exists; dismiss on successful auth
- Logout: call /auth/logout, clear cookies, return to LoginScreen
- Handle session expiry gracefully (401 → show LoginScreen)

title: Android: Gradle project setup and module structure
description:
Create the Android project that will house the GoRead2 Android app. This is the foundation everything else builds on — no other Android work can begin until the project exists and builds cleanly.

What to do:
- New Kotlin app using Jetpack Compose, targeting API 26+ (Android 8), universal (phone + tablet)
- Module/package structure: app/, feature/feedlist/, feature/articlelist/, feature/articlereader/, feature/auth/, feature/settings/, core/network/, core/model/
- Add to the goread2 monorepo under android/ (or a separate repo — decide and document, consistent with the iOS decision in gr-01c4)
- Configure build variants: debug (local backend) and release (production)
- Add a placeholder MainActivity/ComposeScreen that shows 'GoRead2' so the project builds and runs on emulator

title: Android: Feed list screen with unread counts
description:
The primary navigation screen: a list of the user's subscribed feeds with live unread counts.

What to build:
- FeedListScreen: LazyColumn of Feed rows showing title and unread count badge
- 'All Articles' row at the top (shows total unread across all feeds)
- Pull-to-refresh (Compose pull-refresh) calls /cron/refresh-feeds (manual trigger)
- Tap a feed → navigates to ArticleListScreen for that feed

title: Android: Article reader screen (WebView) and article actions
description:
The article reading experience: full article content rendered in WebView, with star/read actions.

What to build:
- ArticleReaderScreen: WebView loading article.content (HTML) with a stylesheet that matches the app's typography and respects dark theme
- Marks article as read via POST /api/articles/:id/read on display
- Toolbar: star/unstar button (POST /api/articles/:id/star), open-in-browser button (Custom Tab), share button (Android share sheet)
- Navigation: back gesture/button to article list; previous/next article buttons in toolbar
- Mark-all-read: available from article list toolbar (POST /api/feeds/all/read)
- Swipe actions on article list rows: star (leading), mark read/unread (trailing)

title: Native Android app (Jetpack Compose) consuming existing REST API
description:
## Why

Extend goread2 to Android with a native app, mirroring the iOS/iPadOS epic (gr-43g). The backend REST API (/api/*) is already complete and returns JSON — the app is purely a new client. No new backend endpoints are needed beyond minor auth-flow adjustments already validated for iOS.

## What can be reused as-is

- All /api/* REST endpoints (feeds, articles, unread counts, star/read, OPML import/export, stats, subscription)
- Google OAuth flow — Android will use Custom Tabs (AppAuth or manual Custom Tabs + redirect) to complete the same /auth/login → /auth/callback flow, landing a session cookie that OkHttp carries via CookieJar
- Stripe subscription gating (the API already returns subscription state; the app respects it)
- All services and database layers — zero changes needed there
- Findings from gr-n8ml (iOS CORS/cookie verification) — Android's OkHttp CookieJar has different defaults than iOS HTTPCookieStorage, so cookie persistence still needs Android-specific verification, but CSRF/CORS findings likely transfer

## What needs to change on the backend

- Likely nothing new — CORS and cookie SameSite policy were already verified compatible with native HTTP clients for iOS (gr-n8ml); Android verification (this epic's first task) should confirm the same holds for OkHttp

## Android app scope (v1)

- Kotlin, Jetpack Compose, targeting Android 8+ (API 26+), phone and tablet
- Custom Tabs (or AppAuth) for Google OAuth login
- OkHttp + CookieJar for API calls (session cookie carries automatically); Retrofit for typed API access
- Screens: feed list with unread counts, article list (paginated, unread filter), article reader (WebView for HTML content), settings/account
- Mark read on scroll / tap, star toggle, mark-all-read
- OPML import (storage access framework) and export (share intent)
- Subscription management (deep-link to Stripe customer portal via existing /api/subscription/portal)
- Offline: cache last-fetched article list in Room or DataStore for read-only offline access

## Out of scope for v1

- Push notifications
- Background sync / WorkManager refresh
- Widgets
- Wear OS

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] User can log in with Google and stay logged in across app launches
- [ ] Feed list loads with correct unread counts
- [ ] Articles paginate correctly (next-cursor) and respect unread filter
- [ ] Mark read, star, and mark-all-read update server state
- [ ] Article reader renders HTML content legibly on phone and tablet
- [ ] OPML import and export work via system share intent / document picker
- [ ] Subscription state is reflected (free vs paid feature gating matches web)
design: Use a single shared NetworkClient (Retrofit + OkHttp with a persistent CookieJar) that all API calls flow through. Model types (Feed, Article, etc.) mirror the JSON the existing handlers already emit — no custom serialization work on the server. WebView for article body rendering reuses the same HTML the web app delivers from entity.Content.
notes: The /api/* routes already require auth via RequireAuth middleware and return JSON. The Android app is purely additive — it is a new consumer of the existing API surface, not a new feature in the backend. Mirrors the structure of the iOS epic (gr-43g); see its 10 child tasks for the equivalent breakdown.

title: iOS: Article reader screen (WKWebView) and article actions
description:
The article reading experience: full article content rendered in WKWebView, with star/read actions.

What to build:

title: iOS: Google OAuth login via ASWebAuthenticationSession
description:
Implement the full authentication flow so users can sign in with Google and stay signed in across app launches.

What to build:
- Call /auth/login to get the Google auth URL, then open it in ASWebAuthenticationSession
- Handle the OAuth callback redirect (custom URL scheme or universal link) to /auth/callback
- On success the session cookie is set — store it in HTTPCookieStorage and persist across launches via HTTPCookieStorage.shared with cookieAcceptPolicy = .always
- Call /api/me after login to confirm session and fetch CSRF token
- LoginView shown when no session exists; dismiss on successful auth
- Logout: call /auth/logout, clear cookies, return to LoginView
- Handle session expiry gracefully (401 → show LoginView)

title: iOS: NetworkClient — URLSession, API models, error handling
description:
Build the shared networking layer all feature screens will use. Getting this right avoids N different ad-hoc URLSession usages across the codebase.

What to build:

- Async/await methods for each API endpoint: getFeeds, getArticles(feedID:cursor:unreadOnly:), markRead, toggleStar, markAllRead, getUnreadCounts, getAccountStats, getSubscriptionInfo, importOPML, exportOPML, createPortalSession
- Swift model types mirroring the JSON the existing handlers emit: Feed, Article, PaginatedArticles (articles + next_cursor), UnreadCounts, AccountStats, SubscriptionInfo
- Typed error enum (NetworkError: unauthorized, notFound, serverError, noConnection, decodingError)
- CSRF token storage and automatic injection into mutating requests via X-CSRF-Token header (fetched from /api/me on login)

title: iOS: Verify backend CORS and cookie compatibility with URLSession
description:
Before building any screens, confirm the existing backend works correctly when called from a native URLSession client rather than a browser. A failed assumption here would break auth or API calls for every screen downstream.

What to verify:
1. Session cookies: confirm HTTPCookieStorage.shared receives and replays the session cookie from /auth/callback correctly (SameSite=Lax should be fine with same-domain; document if any change needed)
2. CSRF: the iOS app will need a CSRF token for mutating requests (POST/DELETE) — verify /api/me returns csrf_token and that the X-CSRF-Token header is accepted from URLSession

title: Article view doesn't scroll to top when navigating with j/k shortcuts
description:
When reading an article scrolled partway down, pressing j to move to the next article displays it at the same scroll offset instead of starting from the top. The scroll position from the previous article is carried over to the next one.

Steps to reproduce:
1. Open any article and scroll down
2. Press j to move to the next article
3. Observe: next article is displayed mid-scroll instead of at the top

Expected: navigating to a new article with j/k always resets the scroll position to the top.
close_reason: Fixed: displayArticle was calling scrollTop=0 on #article-content (inner div) instead of .content-pane (the actual overflow-y:auto scrollable parent). Now uses closest('.content-pane') to reset the right element.
